sections.heading: What is DocJSON
sections.content:
DocJSON is the source format for documentation in Cortex. Each document is a `.json` file stored in the `docs/` directory of a project. When you run `cortex_build`, all JSON files in `docs/` are scanned, indexed as `document` nodes in the graph, and linked to any code nodes they reference.

DocJSON is the *source of truth* — not the rendered markdown. The rendered output (`cortex_render_doc`) is generated on demand from the live index, so linked node summaries always reflect the current state of the code.

sections.heading: File Location and Naming
sections.content:
Place JSON files in the `docs/` directory at the project root:

```
my_project/
  docs/
    architecture.json
    mcp-tools.json
    prds/
      my-feature.json
  .cortex/
    cortex.db
```

The file path relative to `docs/` determines the canonical node ID. The path segments become a dot-separated path: `{project_id}::docs.{dotpath}`.

| File | Node ID |
|---|---|
| `docs/architecture.json` | `myproject::docs.architecture` |
| `docs/mcp-tools.json` | `myproject::docs.mcp-tools` |
| `docs/prds/my-feature.json` | `myproject::docs.prds.my-feature` |

Use lowercase with hyphens for filenames — no spaces or underscores.

sections.heading: Top-Level Structure
sections.content:
Every DocJSON file must have two required keys:

```json
{
  "title": "My Document Title",
  "sections": [ ... ]
}
```

And one optional key:

```json
{
  "tags": ["optional", "list", "of", "tags"]
}
```

The document's canonical identity is derived from its **file path** relative to `docs/`, not from any field in the JSON. There is no top-level `id` key.

| Key | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `title` | yes | Human-readable title. Becomes the H1 in rendered output. |
| `sections` | yes | Array of section objects (see below). Can be empty `[]`. |
| `tags` | no | String array. Stored in the index for filtering via `cortex_list`. |

sections.heading: Section Structure
sections.content:
Each entry in `sections` is an object with two required keys and three optional keys:

```json
{
  "id": "my-section",
  "heading": "My Section Heading",
  "content": "Prose text for this section.",
  "level": 2,
  "tags": ["optional"],
  "links": [
    {"node_id": "myproject::mymodule::my_function"}
  ]
}
```

| Key | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | yes | Slug for this section. Combined with the doc path-derived id: `{project}::docs.{path}::{section_id}`. Lowercase, hyphens only. |
| `heading` | yes | Section heading text. Rendered as `##` by default. |
| `content` | no | Prose content. Supports markdown formatting. Stored in the index as `level_2`. |
| `level` | no | Heading level 2–6 (default 2). Controls the `##` depth in rendered output. |
| `tags` | no | String array. Tags on the section node. |
| `links` | no | Array of `{"node_id": "..."}` objects linking this section to code nodes. |

sections.heading: Nested Sections
sections.content:
Sections can recursively contain sub-sections via an optional `sections` key. This lets you break large sections into smaller, focused pieces without splitting into a separate document.

```json
{
  "id": "database-layer",
  "heading": "Database Layer",
  "content": "Overview of the DB design.",
  "sections": [
    {
      "id": "tables",
      "heading": "Tables",
      "content": "Core table definitions..."
    },
    {
      "id": "migrations",
      "heading": "Migrations",
      "content": "How migrations work..."
    }
  ]
}
```

**Depth limit:** Maximum 3 levels (depth 0, 1, 2). The scanner warns and ignores `sections` on depth-2 nodes. If you need more depth, that's a signal to split into a separate document.

**Node IDs:** Nested sections use dot-path IDs. Given a doc at `docs/architecture.json` in project `cortex`:

| What | Node ID | Depth |
|---|---|---|
| Top-level section | `cortex::docs.architecture::database-layer` | 0 |
| Child section | `cortex::docs.architecture::database-layer.tables` | 1 |
| Grandchild section | `cortex::docs.architecture::database-layer.tables.nodes-table` | 2 |

**Backward compatible:** A section without a `sections` key is a leaf — exactly like the current format. Existing documents work with zero changes.

**Links and staleness:** Links (`links` array) work at any depth. A parent section is marked `LINKED_STALE` when any child section is stale, so you can trace staleness down the hierarchy.

**Heading levels:** Depth auto-maps to heading level: depth 0 → `##`, depth 1 → `###`, depth 2 → `####`. An explicit `level` in the JSON overrides this default.

sections.heading: How Node IDs Work
sections.content:
Node IDs follow the pattern `{project_id}::{dotpath}` where `project_id` defaults to the project root directory name.

Examples for a project named `myproject`:

| What | Node ID |
|---|---|
| Module `myproject/core/db.py` | `myproject::myproject.core.db` |
| Function `upsert_node` in that module | `myproject::myproject.core.db::upsert_node` |
| Markdown doc `docs/architecture.md` | `myproject::docs.architecture` |
| H2 section "Overview" in that doc | `myproject::docs.architecture#overview` |
| JSON doc `docs/design.json` | `myproject::docs.design` |
| JSON doc `docs/prds/my-feature.json` | `myproject::docs.prds.my-feature` |
| Section `database-layer` in design doc | `myproject::docs.design::database-layer` |

JSON doc node IDs are always derived from the file path relative to `docs/`, not from any field inside the JSON.

To find node IDs for your project, use `cortex_list` or `cortex_search`. If you link to a node ID that doesn't exist in the index, `cortex_build` won't fail — it will register the edge against whatever node is scanned later.

sections.heading: Linking Sections to Code
sections.content:
Links connect a doc section to code nodes via a `documents` edge in the graph. This is how the rendered output knows what to show under **Linked nodes** and how `cortex_check` knows which sections to mark as `LINKED_STALE` when the linked code changes.

Add links in two ways:

**1. In the JSON directly:**
```json
"links": [
  {"node_id": "cortex::cortex.index.db::upsert_node"},
  {"node_id": "cortex::cortex.index.db::upsert_edge"}
]
```

**2. Using `cortex_add_link` after the fact:**
```
cortex_add_link(
  project_root = "/path/to/project",
  section_id   = "cortex::docs.my-doc::my-section",
  node_id      = "cortex::cortex.index.db::upsert_node"
)
```
This mutates the JSON file and re-indexes automatically.

sections.heading: What to Link (and What Not To)
sections.content:
Every link is a staleness trigger — when the linked node changes, `cortex_check` flags the doc section as `LINKED_STALE`. This is the whole point: the doc stays honest. But over-linking creates noise.

### The decision test

`cortex_check` is hash-based. *Any* change to a linked function's body flips the stale flag — it cannot distinguish a signature tweak from an internal refactor. So the real question is: **does this section describe something about the function's contract that a code change could invalidate?**

Contract means more than the function signature. Three levels of change can make a doc wrong:

| Change level | Example | Doc impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Signature** | `run_pipeline` adds a required `--timeout` param | CLI examples in the doc are now incomplete |
| **Type / structure** | `_parse_return` stops accepting a bare `DataFrame`, now requires a dict | The return-patterns table is wrong |
| **Behavioral contract** | `pre_run` no longer does git-commit checks | The sequence diagram saying 'git check + cache lookup' is wrong |

**Quick test:** read the section text, then imagine a developer rewrites the linked function. Would someone need to re-read this section to check it's still accurate? If yes → link. If no → skip.

### Link these

- **Public entry points** called out by name in the prose (e.g. `run_pipeline`, `load_pipeline`)
- **Decorators and classes** the section teaches users to use (e.g. `@pm_method`, `RunContext`)
- **Functions whose contract is explicitly documented** in the section — even internal ones, if the section spells out their inputs, outputs, types, or behavior (e.g. `_parse_return` when the section lists its five return patterns — adding a sixth pattern means the doc is incomplete)
- **Cross-doc references** to ADRs or other docs mentioned in the section

### Do NOT link these

- **Private helpers** whose internals the section never describes (`_hardlink_tree`, `_write_meta_json`). A refactor inside them won't invalidate the doc.
- **Whole modules** when the section just mentions the filename for orientation. Every internal change would flag it.
- **Functions owned by a different subfeature's doc.** If `check_cache` is documented in the caching design doc, don't also link it from the execution doc — unless the execution doc explains its behavior.
- **Troubleshooting sections** that describe user-facing symptoms rather than specific function contracts. Linking `topo_sort_steps` to a 'Cycle detected' tip means the tip gets flagged every time the sort algorithm is tweaked, even if the error message hasn't changed.

sections.heading: Content Formatting
sections.content:
The `content` field is plain text that supports markdown. Common patterns:

**Inline code:** wrap with backticks — `` `my_function(x)` ``

**Code blocks:** use triple backtick fences:
```
"content": "Example:\n\n```python\ndef foo():\n    pass\n```"
```

**Tables:** standard markdown table syntax works in content.

**Escaping:** because `content` is a JSON string, newlines are `\n` and backslashes are `\\`.

**Length:** no hard limit, but keep `level_2` (the content stored in the index) meaningful for search. The first sentence is used as the node summary in `cortex_list` output.

sections.heading: Diagrams (Mermaid)
sections.content:
Cortex Viz renders Mermaid diagrams embedded in section content. To add a diagram, use a fenced mermaid code block as the **entire** content of a section:

```json
{
  "id": "system-flow",
  "heading": "System Flow",
  "content": "```mermaid\nflowchart TD\n    A[Input] --> B[Process]\n    B --> C[Output]\n```"
}
```

**Key constraint — one section, one type.** A section should contain *either* prose text *or* a mermaid diagram, not both. Mixing prose and a mermaid block in the same section will cause problems:

- The **Diagram Editor** (◈ button or double-click) replaces the full section content, so surrounding prose would be lost.
- The **Rich Text Editor** (double-click on prose sections) does not understand mermaid blocks and will corrupt the diagram source.

If you need explanatory text alongside a diagram, use two adjacent sections — one for the prose and one for the diagram:

```json
[
  {
    "id": "architecture-overview",
    "heading": "Architecture Overview",
    "content": "The system follows a three-layer architecture. See the diagram below for the full data flow."
  },
  {
    "id": "architecture-diagram",
    "heading": "Architecture Diagram",
    "content": "```mermaid\nflowchart TD\n    A[API] --> B[Service Layer]\n    B --> C[Database]\n```"
  }
]
```

**Editing diagrams in Viz:** Click the ◈ button in the section header (or double-click a rendered diagram) to open the split-pane Diagram Editor with Monaco syntax highlighting on the left and a live preview on the right. Click **Apply** to save changes back to the DocJSON file.

**Supported diagram types:** flowchart, sequence, class, state, ER, gantt, pie, mindmap, timeline, and all other Mermaid v11 diagram types.

sections.heading: Full Example
Authoring Workflow
sections.content:
1. Create a new `.json` file in `docs/` following the format above
2. Run `cortex_build` to index it
3. Use `cortex_render_doc` to see the rendered output and check linked node summaries are correct
4. Use `cortex_list_undocumented` to find code nodes not yet covered by any doc section
5. Add `links` to connect sections to those nodes, then rebuild
6. Use `cortex_check` to monitor whether linked sections go stale as code evolves

### Known limitation: cortex_list does not enumerate doc sections by parent_id

The scanner emits each section as a `document` node with a `composes` edge from the file doc node to the section. In theory, `cortex_list(parent_id="myproject::docs.my-doc")` should return all sections of that document — but in practice it returns 0 results. The `parent_id` traversal appears to only work for code nodes (`atomic_process` / `composite_process`), not `document` nodes.

**Workaround:** To find section IDs for use with `cortex_update_section`, read the JSON file directly — section IDs are the `"id"` fields in the `sections` array. The full qualified section ID is `{project_id}::docs.{dotpath}::{section_id}`.

This is a known gap in `cortex_list`. Until fixed, do not rely on `parent_id` lookups for doc nodes."
